steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A student says “V = IR is Ohm’s Law.” Is this correct?
options: Yes — V = IR is always Ohm’s Law
No — V = IR is the definition of resistance. Ohm’s Law is that current is proportional to p.d. at constant temperature
No — Ohm’s Law only applies to parallel circuits
Yes — but only for series circuits
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! V = IR defines resistance for ANY component. Ohm’s Law is a specific statement: for an ohmic conductor at constant temperature, current is directly proportional to potential difference. Not all components obey Ohm’s Law (e.g. filament lamps, diodes).
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! V = IR defines resistance. Ohm’s Law states that current is directly proportional to p.d. at constant temperature — this only applies to ohmic conductors like fixed resistors at constant temperature.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.2.1 — Ohm’s Law and I-V characteristics

steps.title: Ohm’s Law
steps.bodyHTML: Ohm’s Law states that the current through an ohmic conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it, provided the temperature remains constant. An ohmic conductor (e.g. a fixed resistor at constant temperature) gives a straight-line I-V graph through the origin. A filament lamp is non-ohmic — its resistance increases as it heats up, giving a curved I-V graph.

relationships: For an ohmic conductor: double V → double I (R stays constant)
For a filament lamp: double V → less than double I (R increases)

commonErrors: Saying V = IR IS Ohm’s Law (it is the definition of resistance)
Thinking all components obey Ohm’s Law

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Change the voltage and resistance. For an ohmic conductor, notice the straight-line relationship between V and I.

aqa.questionText: A student investigates a fixed resistor. At 4.0 V the current is 0.20 A. At 8.0 V the current is 0.40 A. Show that the resistor obeys Ohm’s Law and calculate its resistance.

steps.label: Compare results
steps.content: Resistance is the same (20 Ω) at both voltages. Current is proportional to p.d.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — stating R is constant or I ∝ V

steps.label: State conclusion
steps.content: The resistor obeys Ohm’s Law because current is directly proportional to p.d. (constant R).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct conclusion with correct resistance value

edexcel.questionText: An ohmic conductor has a resistance of 8.0 Ω. Calculate the current when the p.d. is 12 V.

steps.label: Substitute
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: A resistor has a p.d. of 10 V and carries a current of 2.5 A. Calculate its resistance. State whether this could be an ohmic conductor.

steps.label: Explain
steps.content: Need more data points to determine if it is ohmic — R must remain constant at different voltages.

ocr-21c.questionText: A wire carries a current of 3.0 A when the p.d. is 6.0 V. Calculate the resistance.

steps.label: Apply R = V / I
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A fixed resistor has a p.d. of 15 V and a current of 0.50 A. Calculate the resistance.

steps.label: Apply R = V / I
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

aqa.questionText: A student doubles the voltage across a component and the current also doubles. What can be concluded?

options.label: B
options.text: The component is an ohmic conductor at constant temperature

options.label: C
options.text: The component is a filament lamp

markSchemeSteps: Current is directly proportional to p.d.
This is Ohm’s Law — ohmic conductor at constant temperature ✓
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark proportionality · 1 mark constant R · 1 mark conclusion
aqa.commonMistake: A filament lamp would NOT double current when voltage doubles — its resistance increases with temperature.

edexcel.questionText: A 12 Ω resistor is connected to a 6.0 V battery. What current flows?
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark rearrangement · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: A current of 0.60 A flows through a 20 Ω resistor. What is the p.d.?
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-21c.questionText: A 5.0 Ω resistor has a p.d. of 10 V across it. Calculate the current.
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A 25 Ω resistor carries a current of 0.80 A. Calculate the p.d.
wjec.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

equation.examTip: V = IR defines resistance. Ohm’s Law is specifically that I ∝ V at constant temperature (only for ohmic conductors).

boardNotes.aqa: On AQA formula sheet. Must understand I-V characteristics.
boardNotes.edexcel: On Edexcel equation sheet. Know the difference between V=IR and Ohm’s Law.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: NOT on formula sheet — must be memorised for OCR Gateway. ⚠️
boardNotes.ocr-21c: NOT on formula sheet — must be memorised for OCR 21st Century. ⚠️
boardNotes.wjec: Provided in WJEC exam. Be able to describe I-V graphs for different components.
